title: Dual-Processor Architectural Approaches to Distributed Processing in Embedded Robotics
abstract: This letter presents a peer-level asymmetric dual-MCU architecture that partitions real-time control and best-effort computation across independent microcontrollers with bounded inter-processor communication latency. Experimental results on two reference platforms demonstrate 15-25% reductions in worst-case interrupt and control-loop latency and significantly improved control-loop jitter under load compared to single-MCU baselines.

title: Thermal Coupling as a Source of Sustained Control Degradation in Embedded Robotic Controllers
abstract: This letter experimentally isolates thermal coupling from scheduling effects in an embedded robotic controller executing fixed-priority workloads. Results demonstrate that control-loop drift and interrupt latency degradation in single-MCU systems correlate with thermal throttling rather than scheduler interference. Offloading computational load to a physically separate microcontroller reduces peak junction temperature by up to 28°C and preserves sustained control-loop stability without modifying control algorithms.

title: Adaptive Task Migration for Peer-Level Dual-MCU Embedded Robotics Systems
abstract: This letter presents an adaptive task migration framework that continuously monitors CPU load, thermal conditions, and inter-MCU latency to redistribute tasks in real time without violating control-loop deadlines. Experimental results on two dual-MCU platforms demonstrate a 12% increase in throughput, 23% fewer latency spikes exceeding 5ms, and an 8.3°C reduction in peak coordinator MCU temperature. Task migration overhead remains below 580µs, preserving deterministic control performance.

title: Dual-MCU Adaptive Priority Scheduling (DMAPS): A Mathematical Framework for Real-Time Task Distribution
abstract: We present Dual-MCU Adaptive Priority Scheduling (DMAPS), a novel distributed real-time scheduling algorithm for coordinated microcontroller architectures. DMAPS achieves provable latency bounds while maintaining computational balance across heterogeneous processing units. Experimental validation demonstrates 45-57% latency reduction compared to single-MCU baselines and 17.2% lower latency than the next-best competing algorithm, with 5-10× lower jitter and O(1) amortized complexity.
